name: Diabetes Type 2
overview: Type 2 diabetes is a chronic metabolic condition where the body becomes resistant to insulin or the pancreas doesn't produce enough insulin. Blood sugar levels remain elevated, damaging blood vessels, nerves, kidneys, eyes, and heart over time. It accounts for 90-95% of all diabetes cases worldwide.

quickFacts.deathRank: #9 cause of death globally
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - 58% risk reduction with lifestyle changes

facts: 462 million people are affected globally
India is the Diabetes Capital with 101 million cases
Walking 30 minutes daily reduces risk by 58%
Prediabetes affects 1 in 3 adults
Losing 5-7% body weight can prevent it
People with diabetes have 2x heart disease risk
Can go undiagnosed for years with no symptoms
Intermittent fasting improves insulin sensitivity

prevention: Maintain a healthy weight through balanced diet
Exercise at least 150 minutes per week
Limit refined sugars and processed foods
Get annual blood sugar screening after age 35
Choose whole grains over refined carbohydrates
Stay hydrated with water instead of sugary drinks
management: Monitor blood sugar levels regularly
Take medications as prescribed
Follow a consistent meal schedule
Check feet daily for cuts or sores
Keep A1C levels below 7%
Attend regular eye and kidney checkups
emergency: Seek help if blood sugar exceeds 300 mg/dL
Watch for signs of diabetic ketoacidosis: nausea, vomiting, fruity breath
Carry glucose tablets for hypoglycemia episodes

name: Hypertension
overview: Hypertension is a chronic condition where blood pushes against artery walls with consistently high force, often called the 'silent killer' because it rarely shows symptoms. Normal blood pressure is 120/80 mmHg, while readings consistently at or above 130/80 mmHg indicate hypertension. Left untreated, it damages the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes over years.

quickFacts.deathRank: Contributes to #1 cause of death (heart disease)
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - DASH diet and exercise reduce risk significantly

facts: 1.28 billion adults worldwide have hypertension
Only 1 in 5 people with hypertension have it under control
The DASH diet can lower blood pressure by 11 mmHg
Reducing sodium intake by 1,000 mg/day lowers BP by 5-6 mmHg
Hypertension is the leading risk factor for stroke
46% of adults with hypertension are unaware they have it
Regular exercise can reduce BP by 5-8 mmHg
Potassium-rich foods help counter sodium's effects on blood pressure

prevention: Limit sodium intake to less than 2,300 mg per day
Follow the DASH diet rich in fruits, vegetables, and whole grains
Exercise at least 30 minutes most days
Maintain a healthy weight
Limit alcohol to 1-2 drinks per day
Manage stress through meditation or deep breathing
management: Take blood pressure medications as prescribed
Monitor blood pressure at home regularly
Reduce caffeine intake
Quit smoking
Get adequate sleep of 7-8 hours nightly
Attend regular doctor checkups
emergency: Seek immediate help if BP exceeds 180/120 mmHg
Call emergency services if experiencing chest pain, vision loss, or difficulty breathing
Do not stop medications abruptly without doctor supervision

name: Anxiety Disorder
overview: Anxiety disorders involve persistent, excessive worry and fear about everyday situations that are difficult to control and out of proportion to actual danger. Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD) is the most common form, affecting daily functioning, sleep, and quality of life. It is the most common mental health condition worldwide, but highly treatable with therapy and medication.

quickFacts.deathRank: Leading cause of disability worldwide
quickFacts.preventable: Partially - stress management helps reduce risk

facts: 301 million people worldwide live with anxiety disorders
CBT has a 60-80% success rate for anxiety treatment
Anxiety disorders cost the global economy over $1 trillion annually
Only 36% of people with anxiety receive treatment
Regular exercise is as effective as medication for mild anxiety
Anxiety often co-occurs with depression in 60% of cases
Deep breathing activates the parasympathetic nervous system within 90 seconds
Gut health directly affects anxiety through the gut-brain axis

prevention: Practice daily mindfulness or meditation
Exercise regularly for at least 30 minutes
Limit caffeine and alcohol consumption
Maintain strong social connections
Establish consistent sleep routines
Learn stress management techniques early
management: Consider cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT)
Practice deep breathing exercises: 4-7-8 technique
Keep a worry journal to identify triggers
Take prescribed medications consistently
Limit news and social media exposure
Use grounding techniques during anxious moments
emergency: During a panic attack: focus on slow breathing and grounding
Call a crisis helpline if anxiety becomes overwhelming
Seek emergency care if experiencing chest pain or suicidal thoughts

name: Heart Attack
overview: A heart attack occurs when blood flow to part of the heart muscle is blocked, usually by a blood clot forming in a coronary artery narrowed by plaque buildup. Without quick restoration of blood flow, the heart muscle begins to die within minutes. Survival rates have improved dramatically with early treatment, but it remains a leading cause of death worldwide.

symptoms.name: Pain radiating to arm, jaw, or back

quickFacts.deathRank: #1 cause of death globally
quickFacts.ageRange: Average age 65, but can occur at any age
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - 80% of premature heart attacks are preventable

facts: Someone has a heart attack every 40 seconds in the United States
80% of premature heart attacks are preventable
Women often experience atypical symptoms like jaw pain and fatigue
Aspirin during a heart attack can improve survival by 23%
The golden hour: treatment within 60 minutes saves the most heart muscle
Heart attack risk increases 2-4x for smokers
Monday mornings have the highest rate of heart attacks
Cardiac rehabilitation reduces repeat heart attack risk by 25%

prevention: Quit smoking and avoid secondhand smoke
Maintain healthy cholesterol and blood pressure levels
Exercise regularly with at least 150 minutes per week
Eat a heart-healthy Mediterranean diet
Manage stress and get adequate sleep
management: Take all prescribed heart medications consistently
Attend cardiac rehabilitation programs
Monitor blood pressure and cholesterol regularly
Gradually return to physical activity as advised
Join a heart disease support group
emergency: Call emergency services immediately - do not drive yourself
Chew an aspirin (325 mg) if not allergic
Perform CPR if someone becomes unresponsive and stops breathing

name: Heart Failure
overview: Heart failure is a chronic condition where the heart cannot pump blood efficiently enough to meet the body's needs. Fluid may back up into the lungs, legs, and abdomen causing swelling and breathing difficulties. Despite its name, heart failure does not mean the heart has stopped but rather that it is weakened and struggling to function properly.

quickFacts.deathRank: 50% mortality within 5 years of diagnosis
quickFacts.preventable: Partially - managing risk factors helps
quickFacts.duration: Chronic - progressive condition requiring lifelong care

facts: 64 million people worldwide live with heart failure
Heart failure is the #1 reason for hospitalization in adults over 65
About 50% of patients die within 5 years of diagnosis
Daily weight monitoring can detect fluid buildup early
Heart failure patients should limit sodium to 1,500 mg/day
Exercise training improves quality of life even in heart failure
Modern medications have improved survival rates by 30%
Heart transplant has a 90% 1-year survival rate

prevention: Control high blood pressure with diet and medication
Manage diabetes and cholesterol levels
Quit smoking and limit alcohol
Maintain a healthy weight
Treat sleep apnea promptly
management: Weigh yourself daily and report sudden gains to your doctor
Restrict sodium intake to under 1,500 mg per day
Take all heart failure medications as prescribed
Monitor fluid intake as recommended
Engage in light exercise such as walking
Get annual flu and pneumonia vaccines
emergency: Seek immediate care for sudden weight gain of 3+ pounds in a day
Call emergency if unable to breathe or experiencing chest pain
Go to ER if coughing up pink or frothy mucus

name: High Cholesterol
overview: High cholesterol means excess cholesterol circulating in the blood, particularly LDL ('bad' cholesterol), which builds up as plaque in artery walls. This narrows and hardens arteries, significantly increasing the risk of heart attack and stroke. It has no symptoms on its own and can only be detected through a blood test.
atRisk: Diet high in saturated and trans fats

quickFacts.affectedGlobally: Over 2 billion adults with elevated cholesterol
quickFacts.deathRank: Contributes to 4.4 million deaths annually
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - diet and exercise are highly effective
quickFacts.duration: Chronic - requires ongoing monitoring and management

facts: 39% of adults worldwide have elevated total cholesterol
Oats and barley can reduce LDL cholesterol by 5-10%
Statins reduce heart attack risk by 25-35%
HDL ('good') cholesterol helps remove LDL from arteries
Trans fats raise LDL and lower HDL simultaneously
Familial hypercholesterolemia affects 1 in 250 people
Cholesterol levels can be checked with a simple blood test
Nuts like almonds and walnuts lower LDL cholesterol

prevention: Eat a diet rich in fruits, vegetables, and whole grains
Replace saturated fats with healthy unsaturated fats
Avoid trans fats found in processed foods
Exercise at least 150 minutes per week
Get cholesterol levels checked every 4-6 years starting at 20
management: Take statin medication as prescribed by your doctor
Increase soluble fiber intake with oats, beans, and lentils
Add omega-3 fatty acids from fish or supplements
Quit smoking to improve HDL cholesterol
Maintain a healthy weight
Monitor cholesterol levels regularly
emergency: Seek immediate help for chest pain or pressure
Watch for signs of stroke: sudden numbness, confusion, trouble speaking

name: Stroke
overview: A stroke occurs when blood supply to part of the brain is interrupted or reduced, depriving brain tissue of oxygen and nutrients. Brain cells begin dying within minutes. There are two main types: ischemic (caused by a blood clot, 87% of cases) and hemorrhagic (caused by a burst blood vessel). Quick treatment using the FAST method is critical to survival and recovery.

symptoms.name: Sudden face drooping on one side
Vision loss in one or both eyes
Difficulty walking or loss of balance

quickFacts.deathRank: #2 cause of death globally
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - 80% of strokes are preventable
quickFacts.duration: Acute - recovery can take months to years

facts: Stroke is the #2 cause of death worldwide, killing 6.5 million annually
80% of strokes are preventable through lifestyle changes
Every minute during a stroke, 1.9 million brain cells die
tPA clot-busting treatment must be given within 4.5 hours
FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call
Women have higher lifetime risk of stroke than men
High blood pressure is the single greatest risk factor for stroke
Stroke survivors have a 25% chance of having another within 5 years

prevention: Control blood pressure through diet and medication
Quit smoking and avoid secondhand smoke
Manage diabetes and cholesterol levels
Exercise regularly for cardiovascular health
Treat atrial fibrillation with prescribed anticoagulants
Limit alcohol consumption
management: Begin rehabilitation as soon as medically stable
Take prescribed blood thinners or antiplatelet drugs
Attend physical, occupational, and speech therapy
Monitor and control blood pressure daily
Screen for depression after stroke
emergency: Remember FAST: Face, Arms, Speech, Time - call emergency immediately
Note the exact time symptoms started for treatment decisions
Do not give the person food or water as swallowing may be impaired

name: Arrhythmia
overview: Arrhythmia is a condition where the heart beats too fast, too slow, or irregularly due to problems with the electrical signals that coordinate heartbeats. Atrial fibrillation (AFib) is the most common type, affecting millions worldwide. While many arrhythmias are harmless, some can lead to stroke, heart failure, or sudden cardiac death.

quickFacts.deathRank: AFib increases stroke risk 5x
quickFacts.preventable: Partially - managing risk factors helps
quickFacts.duration: Variable - can be episodic or permanent

facts: Atrial fibrillation affects 33.5 million people globally
AFib increases stroke risk by 5 times
The heart beats about 100,000 times per day normally
Catheter ablation can cure certain types of arrhythmia
Smartwatches can now detect irregular heart rhythms
Magnesium deficiency can trigger arrhythmias
Caffeine in moderate amounts does not cause arrhythmia in most people
Vagal maneuvers can sometimes stop supraventricular tachycardia

prevention: Limit caffeine and alcohol consumption
Manage stress through relaxation techniques
Treat underlying conditions like thyroid disease or sleep apnea
Maintain a healthy weight
Avoid stimulant drugs
management: Take antiarrhythmic medications as prescribed
Use blood thinners if prescribed for AFib
Monitor heart rate regularly
Avoid known triggers like excessive caffeine
Consider catheter ablation for recurring episodes
Wear a medical alert bracelet
emergency: Seek immediate care for fainting, chest pain, or severe shortness of breath
Call emergency if heart rate exceeds 150 bpm at rest
Learn vagal maneuvers to attempt to slow rapid heart rate

name: Diabetes Type 1
overview: Type 1 diabetes is an autoimmune condition where the immune system mistakenly destroys insulin-producing beta cells in the pancreas. Without insulin, the body cannot regulate blood sugar, requiring lifelong insulin injections or an insulin pump. It typically develops in children and young adults and accounts for 5-10% of all diabetes cases.

quickFacts.deathRank: Life-threatening without insulin
quickFacts.preventable: No - autoimmune condition

facts: 8.4 million people live with type 1 diabetes worldwide
Type 1 diabetes is not caused by diet or lifestyle
Before insulin discovery in 1921, T1D was a death sentence
The incidence of T1D is increasing by 3-5% per year globally
Continuous glucose monitors have revolutionized T1D management
Artificial pancreas technology is improving outcomes significantly
T1D patients need insulin to survive - it cannot be managed with pills alone
Finland has the highest rate of T1D in the world

prevention: Currently no known way to prevent type 1 diabetes
Research into autoimmune prevention is ongoing
Screen family members for autoantibodies
Maintain awareness of early symptoms for prompt diagnosis
management: Administer insulin via injections or insulin pump
Monitor blood glucose levels multiple times daily
Count carbohydrates to match insulin doses
Carry fast-acting glucose for hypoglycemia
Attend regular endocrinologist appointments
Use continuous glucose monitoring systems if available
emergency: Treat low blood sugar immediately with 15g fast-acting glucose
Seek emergency care for diabetic ketoacidosis symptoms
Always carry a glucagon emergency kit

name: Hypothyroidism
overview: Hypothyroidism occurs when the thyroid gland does not produce enough thyroid hormones, slowing down the body's metabolism. Hashimoto's thyroiditis, an autoimmune condition, is the most common cause. It leads to fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, and if untreated, can cause serious complications including heart disease and myxedema coma.

quickFacts.deathRank: Rarely fatal with treatment
quickFacts.preventable: No - mostly autoimmune

facts: Hypothyroidism affects up to 5% of the global population
Women are 5-8 times more likely to develop thyroid problems
A simple TSH blood test can diagnose hypothyroidism
Levothyroxine is one of the most prescribed medications worldwide
Iodine deficiency is the most common preventable cause globally
Untreated hypothyroidism can lead to heart disease
Thyroid hormone affects every cell in the body
Many people with mild hypothyroidism are undiagnosed

prevention: Ensure adequate iodine intake through diet
Get thyroid screening if you have family history
Monitor thyroid function during pregnancy
Be aware of medications that affect thyroid function
management: Take levothyroxine on an empty stomach daily
Get TSH levels checked every 6-12 months
Take medication at the same time each day
Wait 30-60 minutes after taking thyroid medication before eating
Avoid taking calcium or iron supplements with thyroid medication
Monitor symptoms and report changes to your doctor
emergency: Seek care for signs of myxedema: extreme fatigue, confusion, hypothermia
Do not stop thyroid medication abruptly

name: Hyperthyroidism
overview: Hyperthyroidism occurs when the thyroid gland produces excessive thyroid hormones, accelerating the body's metabolism. Graves' disease, an autoimmune disorder, is the most common cause. It can cause rapid heart rate, weight loss, anxiety, and tremors, and if untreated, can lead to serious heart problems and thyroid storm.

symptoms.name: Thyroid storm: fever, confusion, rapid pulse

quickFacts.deathRank: Treatable; thyroid storm is life-threatening
quickFacts.preventable: No - primarily autoimmune
quickFacts.duration: Treatable - can be cured with radioiodine or surgery

facts: Graves' disease accounts for 60-80% of hyperthyroidism cases
Women are 5-10 times more likely to develop hyperthyroidism
Radioactive iodine treatment has been used since the 1940s
Untreated hyperthyroidism can cause osteoporosis
Beta-blockers can quickly relieve symptoms while treatment takes effect
Thyroid storm has a mortality rate of 10-30% if untreated
Stress can trigger or worsen Graves' disease
Some cases resolve spontaneously without treatment

prevention: No proven prevention, but avoid excessive iodine
Quit smoking to reduce Graves' disease risk
Manage stress to prevent flare-ups
Regular thyroid screening if family history exists
management: Take antithyroid medications as prescribed
Consider radioactive iodine therapy or surgery
Avoid iodine-rich supplements
Monitor heart rate and weight regularly
Protect eyes from dryness if Graves' eye disease is present
Get regular thyroid function tests
emergency: Seek emergency care for thyroid storm: high fever, rapid pulse, confusion
Call emergency if heart rate exceeds 150 bpm with other symptoms

name: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
overview: PCOS is a hormonal disorder common in women of reproductive age, characterized by irregular periods, excess androgen (male hormone) levels, and polycystic ovaries. It affects metabolism, fertility, and appearance, and is closely linked to insulin resistance. PCOS is the leading cause of infertility in women worldwide.

quickFacts.deathRank: Not directly fatal but increases metabolic risks
quickFacts.preventable: No - but symptoms are manageable

facts: PCOS affects 8-13% of women of reproductive age globally
Up to 70% of women with PCOS remain undiagnosed
PCOS is the leading cause of female infertility
Women with PCOS have a 4x higher risk of type 2 diabetes
Weight loss of just 5% can restore ovulation in some women
PCOS is linked to higher risk of endometrial cancer
Metformin, a diabetes drug, is commonly used to treat PCOS
Inositol supplements have shown promise in managing PCOS symptoms

prevention: Maintain a healthy weight through balanced diet
Exercise regularly to improve insulin sensitivity
Limit refined carbohydrates and sugary foods
Get evaluated early if periods are irregular
management: Take prescribed birth control pills to regulate periods
Use anti-androgen medications for excess hair growth
Follow a low-glycemic diet
Exercise at least 150 minutes per week
Monitor for diabetes and cholesterol regularly
Consider fertility treatments if trying to conceive
emergency: Seek care for severe pelvic pain which may indicate ovarian torsion
Consult a doctor for heavy or prolonged menstrual bleeding

name: Insulin Resistance
overview: Insulin resistance occurs when cells in the body don't respond effectively to insulin, causing the pancreas to produce more insulin to compensate. Over time, this leads to elevated blood sugar and is the precursor to type 2 diabetes. It is closely linked to metabolic syndrome, a cluster of conditions including high blood pressure, obesity, and abnormal cholesterol.
atRisk: Waist circumference over 40 inches (men) or 35 inches (women)

quickFacts.deathRank: Precursor to #9 cause of death (diabetes)
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - through diet and exercise

facts: Up to 1 in 3 adults have insulin resistance or prediabetes
Insulin resistance can be reversed with lifestyle changes
Just 150 minutes of weekly exercise can improve insulin sensitivity by 25%
Sleep deprivation for even one night increases insulin resistance
Visceral fat (belly fat) is the strongest predictor of insulin resistance
Apple cider vinegar may modestly improve insulin sensitivity
Strength training is particularly effective at improving insulin sensitivity
Insulin resistance underlies metabolic syndrome, affecting 25% of adults

prevention: Maintain a healthy weight, especially around the midsection
Exercise regularly, including both cardio and strength training
Eat a diet rich in fiber, vegetables, and lean proteins
Limit refined carbohydrates and added sugars
Get 7-9 hours of quality sleep nightly
Manage stress levels
management: Follow a low-glycemic or Mediterranean diet
Walk for 10-15 minutes after each meal
Take metformin if prescribed by your doctor
Monitor fasting glucose and HbA1c regularly
Reduce processed food consumption
Consider intermittent fasting under medical guidance
emergency: Seek medical advice if fasting blood sugar exceeds 126 mg/dL
Consult a doctor if experiencing frequent urination, extreme thirst, or unexplained weight loss

name: Asthma
overview: Asthma is a chronic respiratory condition where airways become inflamed, narrow, and produce excess mucus, making breathing difficult. Triggers include allergens, exercise, cold air, and respiratory infections. While there is no cure, asthma can be well-controlled with proper medication and trigger avoidance, allowing most patients to live normal, active lives.
atRisk: Family history of asthma or allergies
Living in urban areas with air pollution
Allergic conditions like eczema or hay fever

quickFacts.deathRank: 455,000 deaths per year globally
quickFacts.preventable: No - but attacks are preventable with medication

facts: 262 million people had asthma in 2019 globally
Asthma causes 455,000 deaths annually, most of them preventable
80% of asthma deaths occur in low and lower-middle income countries
Inhaled corticosteroids reduce asthma attacks by 50%
Exercise-induced asthma affects up to 90% of people with asthma
Children with asthma miss 13 million school days per year in the US
Asthma symptoms can change over a lifetime
Cockroach and dust mite allergens are major indoor asthma triggers

prevention: Identify and avoid personal asthma triggers
Use air purifiers and maintain clean indoor air
Get annual flu and pneumonia vaccines
Avoid smoking and secondhand smoke exposure
Cover nose and mouth in cold weather
Use allergen-proof bedding covers
management: Use controller inhaler daily as prescribed
Keep a rescue inhaler accessible at all times
Follow an asthma action plan created with your doctor
Monitor peak flow readings regularly
Rinse mouth after using corticosteroid inhaler
Get regular asthma checkups
emergency: Use rescue inhaler immediately during an attack
Call emergency if inhaler provides no relief within 15 minutes
Seek immediate care for blue lips, inability to speak, or severe distress

name: COPD
overview: COPD is a group of progressive lung diseases, primarily emphysema and chronic bronchitis, that block airflow and make breathing increasingly difficult. Smoking is the leading cause, responsible for up to 90% of cases. COPD is not reversible, but treatment can slow progression and improve quality of life.

quickFacts.deathRank: #3 cause of death globally
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - 90% caused by smoking

facts: COPD is the 3rd leading cause of death worldwide
90% of COPD deaths in low and middle income countries
Quitting smoking at any stage slows COPD progression
COPD costs the US healthcare system $50 billion annually
Pulmonary rehabilitation can significantly improve quality of life
3.23 million people died from COPD in 2019
Many people don't know they have COPD until it's advanced
Supplemental oxygen therapy extends survival in severe COPD

prevention: Never smoke or quit smoking as soon as possible
Avoid secondhand smoke and air pollution
Use protective equipment in dusty or chemical workplaces
Get lung function tests if you have risk factors
Get vaccinated against flu and pneumonia
management: Use bronchodilator inhalers as prescribed
Attend pulmonary rehabilitation programs
Practice breathing exercises like pursed-lip breathing
Stay active with gentle daily exercise
Monitor oxygen levels with a pulse oximeter
Avoid respiratory irritants and cold air
emergency: Seek immediate care for severe breathlessness that doesn't improve
Call emergency if lips or fingernails turn blue
Go to ER for confusion, rapid heartbeat, or inability to catch breath

name: Pneumonia
overview: Pneumonia is an infection that inflames the air sacs in one or both lungs, which may fill with fluid or pus. It can be caused by bacteria, viruses, or fungi. Pneumonia ranges from mild to life-threatening and is most serious for infants, older adults, and people with weakened immune systems or chronic health conditions.

quickFacts.deathRank: Leading infectious cause of death in children
quickFacts.ageRange: Most dangerous in under 5 and over 65
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - through vaccines and hygiene

facts: Pneumonia kills over 2.5 million people annually including 800,000 children
Pneumococcal vaccine can prevent the most common bacterial form
Walking pneumonia is a milder form you can have without knowing
Pneumonia is the leading cause of death in children under 5 worldwide
Hospital-acquired pneumonia is more dangerous than community-acquired
Most healthy adults recover from pneumonia in 1-3 weeks
Hand washing is one of the most effective prevention methods
COVID-19 can cause viral pneumonia in severe cases

prevention: Get pneumococcal and flu vaccines
Wash hands frequently and thoroughly
Don't smoke - smoking damages lung defenses
Practice good oral hygiene
Maintain a strong immune system with proper nutrition
management: Complete the full course of prescribed antibiotics
Rest and drink plenty of fluids
Take over-the-counter medications for fever
Use a humidifier to ease breathing
Follow up with chest X-ray to confirm recovery
emergency: Seek immediate care for difficulty breathing or blue lips
Go to ER for persistent high fever above 102F (39C)
Call emergency if confused or unable to keep fluids down

name: Sleep Apnea
overview: Sleep apnea is a serious sleep disorder where breathing repeatedly stops and starts during sleep. Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), the most common form, occurs when throat muscles relax and block the airway. Untreated sleep apnea increases the risk of heart disease, stroke, diabetes, and daytime accidents due to excessive sleepiness.

quickFacts.deathRank: Increases cardiovascular death risk by 30%
quickFacts.preventable: Partially - weight loss can prevent or cure mild cases

facts: An estimated 936 million adults have mild to severe OSA worldwide
80% of moderate to severe sleep apnea cases are undiagnosed
People with untreated sleep apnea are 3x more likely to have a car accident
CPAP therapy is 95% effective when used consistently
Sleep apnea patients stop breathing up to 30 times per hour
Weight loss of 10% can reduce sleep apnea severity by 50%
Sleeping on your side can reduce mild sleep apnea symptoms
Sleep apnea is linked to resistant hypertension

prevention: Maintain a healthy weight
Avoid alcohol before bedtime
Sleep on your side instead of your back
Quit smoking
Treat nasal congestion or allergies
management: Use CPAP machine consistently every night
Try oral appliances for mild to moderate cases
Lose weight if overweight
Elevate head of bed by 4-6 inches
Avoid sedatives and sleeping pills
Get follow-up sleep studies to adjust treatment
emergency: Seek evaluation if bed partner reports you stop breathing
See a doctor urgently if experiencing excessive daytime sleepiness affecting safety

name: Bronchitis
overview: Bronchitis is inflammation of the bronchial tubes that carry air to and from the lungs. Acute bronchitis is usually caused by viral infections and resolves within 1-3 weeks. Chronic bronchitis, a form of COPD, involves a persistent cough with mucus lasting at least 3 months for 2 consecutive years, usually caused by smoking.
atRisk: Workers exposed to dust or fumes

symptoms.name: Mucus production (clear, white, yellow, or green)

quickFacts.affectedGlobally: One of the most common reasons for doctor visits
quickFacts.deathRank: Rarely fatal in acute form
quickFacts.ageRange: All ages, more severe in elderly and children
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - hand hygiene and avoiding smoke

facts: Acute bronchitis affects about 5% of adults annually
90% of acute bronchitis cases are viral, not bacterial
Antibiotics do not help viral bronchitis
The cough from bronchitis can linger for several weeks after infection clears
Honey is as effective as cough suppressants for bronchitis cough
Chronic bronchitis is defined as cough with mucus for 3+ months
Hand washing prevents most cases of acute bronchitis
Smokers are much more likely to develop chronic bronchitis

prevention: Wash hands frequently during cold and flu season
Don't smoke and avoid secondhand smoke
Get annual flu vaccine
Avoid close contact with sick individuals
Use a mask in dusty or polluted environments
management: Rest and drink plenty of fluids
Use a humidifier to moisten air
Take over-the-counter cough medicine if needed
Try honey and warm liquids to soothe cough
Avoid antibiotics unless bacterial infection is confirmed
emergency: Seek care if cough lasts more than 3 weeks
See a doctor if you cough up blood or have high fever
Go to ER for severe difficulty breathing

name: Acid Reflux / GERD
overview: Acid reflux occurs when stomach acid flows back into the esophagus, causing heartburn and irritation. When this happens frequently (twice a week or more), it is called GERD. Chronic acid reflux can damage the esophageal lining and increase the risk of Barrett's esophagus and esophageal cancer if left untreated.
atRisk: People who eat large meals or eat late at night

symptoms.name: Regurgitation of food or sour liquid
Sensation of a lump in throat

quickFacts.deathRank: Not directly fatal but can lead to cancer
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - through diet and lifestyle changes
quickFacts.duration: Chronic - manageable with medication and lifestyle

facts: GERD affects approximately 20% of the Western population
Elevating the head of the bed by 6 inches reduces nighttime symptoms by 67%
Eating within 3 hours of bedtime worsens acid reflux
PPIs (proton pump inhibitors) are among the most prescribed drugs globally
Chronic GERD increases esophageal cancer risk by 7-8 times
Chewing gum after meals can help by increasing saliva production
Coffee, chocolate, and spicy foods are common triggers
Losing 10% of body weight can significantly reduce symptoms

prevention: Eat smaller, more frequent meals
Avoid eating 3 hours before bedtime
Maintain a healthy weight
Limit trigger foods: coffee, alcohol, spicy and fatty foods
Quit smoking
Wear loose-fitting clothing
management: Take antacids or PPIs as prescribed
Elevate the head of your bed 6-8 inches
Don't lie down immediately after eating
Keep a food diary to identify triggers
Try sleeping on your left side
Consider endoscopy if symptoms persist despite medication
emergency: Seek care if chest pain is severe to rule out heart attack
See a doctor urgently for difficulty swallowing or vomiting blood

name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome
overview: Irritable bowel syndrome is a common functional gastrointestinal disorder characterized by recurring abdominal pain associated with changes in bowel habits. Unlike inflammatory bowel disease, IBS does not cause visible damage to the digestive tract. It is classified as IBS-D (diarrhea), IBS-C (constipation), or IBS-M (mixed), and is strongly influenced by the gut-brain connection.
atRisk: History of physical or sexual abuse

quickFacts.deathRank: Not fatal but severely impacts quality of life
quickFacts.preventable: No - but flares can be prevented

facts: IBS affects 10-15% of the global population
The low-FODMAP diet provides relief for up to 75% of IBS patients
IBS is a disorder of gut-brain interaction
Stress is one of the strongest triggers for IBS flare-ups
IBS does not increase risk of colon cancer
Gut microbiome differences are found in IBS patients
Peppermint oil can reduce IBS symptoms by 40%
Cognitive behavioral therapy is effective for IBS management

prevention: Identify and avoid trigger foods
Manage stress with relaxation techniques
Eat regular meals at consistent times
Exercise regularly to improve gut motility
Get adequate sleep
management: Try the low-FODMAP diet under dietitian guidance
Take probiotics for gut health
Use peppermint oil capsules for cramping
Consider cognitive behavioral therapy
Keep a food and symptom diary
Take prescribed medications for diarrhea or constipation
emergency: See a doctor if there is blood in stool
Seek care for severe pain, unexplained weight loss, or persistent vomiting

name: Fatty Liver Disease
overview: Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease occurs when excess fat builds up in the liver without significant alcohol consumption. It ranges from simple steatosis (fat accumulation) to NASH (non-alcoholic steatohepatitis) which involves inflammation and can progress to cirrhosis. It is closely linked to obesity, diabetes, and metabolic syndrome.

symptoms.name: Usually no symptoms in early stage

quickFacts.affectedGlobally: Over 1 billion people (25% of global population)
quickFacts.deathRank: Leading cause of liver transplants in coming decades
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - through weight management and diet
quickFacts.duration: Reversible in early stages with lifestyle changes

facts: NAFLD affects approximately 25% of the global population
It is the most common chronic liver disease worldwide
NAFLD can progress to cirrhosis and liver cancer
Weight loss of 7-10% can reverse early fatty liver
There are currently no FDA-approved medications specifically for NAFLD
Children can develop fatty liver disease from childhood obesity
Coffee consumption may protect against fatty liver progression
NAFLD is now being renamed to MASLD (Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease)

prevention: Maintain a healthy weight through diet and exercise
Limit sugar, especially fructose from sugary drinks
Eat a Mediterranean diet rich in healthy fats
Exercise at least 150 minutes per week
Limit alcohol consumption
Manage diabetes and cholesterol
management: Lose 7-10% of body weight gradually
Eliminate sugary beverages and processed foods
Take vitamin E supplements if recommended by doctor
Monitor liver enzymes regularly
Moderate coffee consumption may be beneficial
Get hepatitis A and B vaccinations
emergency: Seek care for jaundice, severe abdominal swelling, or confusion
Consult a hepatologist if liver enzymes remain elevated

name: Gastritis
overview: Gastritis is inflammation of the stomach lining, which can occur suddenly (acute) or gradually (chronic). Common causes include H. pylori bacterial infection, excessive use of NSAIDs like ibuprofen, and excessive alcohol consumption. If untreated, chronic gastritis can lead to stomach ulcers and increase the risk of stomach cancer.
atRisk: Stress from severe illness or surgery

quickFacts.affectedGlobally: Over 50% of world population has H. pylori
quickFacts.deathRank: Rarely fatal but can lead to ulcers and cancer
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - avoiding irritants and treating H. pylori
quickFacts.duration: Variable - acute resolves in days; chronic needs treatment

facts: H. pylori infects about 50% of the world's population
Most people with H. pylori never develop symptoms
NSAIDs are the second most common cause of gastritis
H. pylori can be eradicated with a 2-week antibiotic course
Chronic gastritis increases stomach cancer risk by 2-6 times
Stress from major illness can cause acute erosive gastritis
Probiotics may help treat H. pylori alongside antibiotics
Autoimmune gastritis can lead to vitamin B12 deficiency

prevention: Limit NSAID use and take them with food
Avoid excessive alcohol consumption
Quit smoking
Practice good hygiene to prevent H. pylori infection
Eat smaller meals more frequently
management: Take prescribed PPIs or antacids
Complete H. pylori eradication therapy if diagnosed
Avoid spicy, acidic, and fried foods during flares
Eat bland foods during acute episodes
Reduce stress through relaxation techniques
Stop NSAIDs and switch to acetaminophen if possible
emergency: Seek immediate care for vomiting blood or black stools
Go to ER for severe abdominal pain with dizziness

name: Constipation
overview: Constipation is a common digestive condition characterized by infrequent bowel movements (fewer than 3 per week), hard or dry stools, and difficulty passing stool. It can be caused by low fiber diet, dehydration, lack of exercise, medications, or underlying conditions. While usually not serious, chronic constipation can significantly impact quality of life.

symptoms.name: Fewer than 3 bowel movements per week

quickFacts.affectedGlobally: Affects 16% of adults globally, 33% of those over 60
quickFacts.deathRank: Rarely fatal but causes significant discomfort
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - with proper diet, hydration, and exercise

facts: Approximately 16% of adults worldwide experience constipation
Fiber intake of 25-30g daily prevents most cases of constipation
Drinking 8 glasses of water daily helps prevent constipation
The squatting position is more natural for bowel movements than sitting
Constipation costs the US healthcare system $235 million annually in ER visits
Opioid-induced constipation affects 40-80% of pain medication users
Probiotics can improve stool frequency in chronic constipation
Exercise increases gut motility and helps prevent constipation

prevention: Eat 25-30 grams of fiber daily from fruits, vegetables, and whole grains
Drink 8 or more glasses of water daily
Exercise regularly to stimulate bowel movements
Don't ignore the urge to have a bowel movement
Limit processed foods and dairy if sensitive
Establish a regular bathroom routine
management: Try osmotic laxatives like polyethylene glycol
Add psyllium husk or fiber supplements gradually
Use a footstool to elevate feet while sitting on toilet
Try abdominal massage in clockwise direction
Consider probiotic supplements
Discuss medication changes if drugs are causing constipation
emergency: Seek care if unable to pass stool or gas for several days
Go to ER for severe abdominal pain with no bowel movement

name: Migraine
overview: Migraine is a neurological condition causing intense, throbbing headaches typically on one side of the head, often accompanied by nausea, vomiting, and extreme sensitivity to light and sound. Attacks can last 4-72 hours and can be debilitating. About 25% of migraine sufferers experience aura, visual or sensory disturbances that precede the headache.
atRisk: Certain foods and drinks as triggers

quickFacts.deathRank: #2 cause of disability worldwide
quickFacts.preventable: Partially - triggers can be identified and avoided

facts: Migraine affects over 1 billion people globally
It is the 2nd leading cause of disability worldwide
CGRP inhibitors are a new class of migraine-specific preventive drugs
Women are 3 times more likely to get migraines due to hormonal factors
90% of migraine sufferers cannot function normally during an attack
Migraine with aura slightly increases stroke risk
Botox injections are FDA-approved for chronic migraine prevention
A migraine is not just a headache - it is a complex neurological event

prevention: Keep a headache diary to identify triggers
Maintain regular sleep schedules
Stay hydrated and don't skip meals
Exercise regularly but avoid overexertion
Consider preventive medications if migraines are frequent
Manage stress with relaxation techniques
management: Take acute medications (triptans) at first sign of attack
Rest in a dark, quiet room during an attack
Apply cold or hot compresses to head or neck
Try CGRP inhibitor medications for prevention
Consider Botox for chronic migraine (15+ days/month)
Practice biofeedback or acupuncture
emergency: Seek immediate care for the worst headache of your life
Go to ER if headache is accompanied by fever, stiff neck, or confusion
Call emergency for sudden headache with vision loss or weakness

name: Alzheimer's Disease
overview: Alzheimer's disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder and the most common cause of dementia, accounting for 60-80% of cases. It causes brain cells to degenerate and die, leading to a continuous decline in memory, thinking, and behavioral skills. There is currently no cure, though treatments can temporarily slow symptom progression.

symptoms.name: Problems with words in speaking or writing

quickFacts.deathRank: #7 cause of death globally
quickFacts.ageRange: Risk doubles every 5 years after age 65
quickFacts.preventable: Partially - healthy lifestyle may delay onset
quickFacts.duration: Chronic progressive - average 4-8 years after diagnosis

facts: 55 million people live with dementia worldwide, mostly Alzheimer's
Alzheimer's is the 7th leading cause of death globally
The disease can begin developing 20 years before symptoms appear
Two-thirds of Alzheimer's patients are women
Cardiovascular health in midlife strongly influences dementia risk
Bilingualism may delay Alzheimer's onset by 4-5 years
Caregivers of Alzheimer's patients face high rates of depression and burnout
New amyloid-targeting drugs show promise in slowing early-stage disease

prevention: Stay mentally active with reading, puzzles, and learning
Exercise regularly for cardiovascular and brain health
Follow a Mediterranean or MIND diet
Maintain social connections and engagement
Manage cardiovascular risk factors
Get adequate sleep and treat sleep disorders
management: Take prescribed cholinesterase inhibitors or memantine
Establish daily routines and use memory aids
Ensure a safe living environment to prevent falls
Join support groups for patients and caregivers
Plan for legal and financial matters early
Consider adult day programs for stimulation
emergency: Seek care if patient becomes aggressive or severely confused
Call for help if patient wanders and cannot be found
Contact emergency services for falls or injuries

name: Epilepsy
overview: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder characterized by recurrent, unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal electrical activity in the brain. Seizures can range from brief lapses of attention to severe, prolonged convulsions. With proper medication, about 70% of people with epilepsy can become seizure-free.

symptoms.name: Uncontrollable jerking of arms and legs
Fear, anxiety, or deja vu before seizure

quickFacts.deathRank: 3x higher premature death risk than general population
quickFacts.preventable: Some causes preventable (head injury, infections)
quickFacts.duration: Chronic - lifelong in most cases, some childhood forms resolve

facts: Epilepsy affects 50 million people worldwide
70% of epilepsy patients can become seizure-free with medication
In 50% of cases, the cause of epilepsy is unknown
A seizure lasting more than 5 minutes is a medical emergency
The ketogenic diet was developed in the 1920s to treat epilepsy
Epilepsy surgery can cure certain types of focal epilepsy
Flashing lights only trigger seizures in about 3% of epilepsy patients
SUDEP (sudden unexpected death in epilepsy) affects 1 in 1,000 patients per year

prevention: Wear seatbelts and helmets to prevent head injuries
Get proper prenatal care to reduce birth complications
Treat infections promptly to prevent brain damage
Avoid excessive alcohol and drug use
management: Take anti-seizure medications consistently at the same times
Get adequate sleep - sleep deprivation triggers seizures
Avoid alcohol as it can trigger seizures
Wear a medical alert bracelet
Consider the ketogenic diet if medications aren't sufficient
Track seizures in a diary to identify patterns
emergency: During a seizure: turn person on their side, do NOT put anything in mouth
Call emergency if seizure lasts more than 5 minutes
Seek immediate care for first-time seizure or repeated seizures

name: Sciatica
overview: Sciatica refers to pain that radiates along the path of the sciatic nerve, from the lower back through the hips, buttocks, and down each leg. It is usually caused by a herniated disc, bone spur, or spinal stenosis compressing part of the nerve. While the pain can be severe, most cases resolve within a few weeks with conservative treatment.
atRisk: Jobs requiring heavy lifting or prolonged sitting

symptoms.name: Sharp pain shooting down the leg
Numbness or tingling in the leg
Loss of bladder or bowel control

quickFacts.affectedGlobally: Affects up to 40% of people at some point
quickFacts.deathRank: Not fatal but major cause of disability
quickFacts.preventable: Partially - through proper posture and exercise

facts: Up to 40% of people experience sciatica at some point in life
The sciatic nerve is the longest and thickest nerve in the body
Most sciatica cases resolve without surgery within 6 weeks
Bed rest actually worsens sciatica - gentle movement is better
Walking is one of the best exercises for sciatica
MRI scans often show disc abnormalities in people without pain
Surgery is needed in only about 5-10% of sciatica cases
McKenzie exercises are specifically designed for sciatica relief

prevention: Maintain good posture when sitting and standing
Strengthen core muscles to support the spine
Use proper lifting technique - bend at the knees
Take breaks from prolonged sitting every 30 minutes
Maintain a healthy weight
Sleep on a supportive mattress
management: Apply ice for the first 48-72 hours, then switch to heat
Take anti-inflammatory medications as directed
Do gentle stretching exercises daily
Try physical therapy for targeted exercises
Consider epidural steroid injections for severe pain
Continue gentle activity - avoid complete bed rest
emergency: Seek immediate care for loss of bladder or bowel control (cauda equina syndrome)
Go to ER for sudden severe weakness in the legs

name: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
overview: Carpal tunnel syndrome occurs when the median nerve, which runs from the forearm through a narrow passageway in the wrist (carpal tunnel), becomes compressed. This causes numbness, tingling, and weakness in the hand and fingers. It is often related to repetitive hand motions and is one of the most common nerve compression disorders.
atRisk: Repetitive hand movement workers (typing, assembly line)

symptoms.name: Numbness and tingling in thumb, index, and middle fingers
Muscle wasting at base of thumb

quickFacts.deathRank: Not fatal; leading cause of hand disability
quickFacts.preventable: Partially - ergonomic modifications help
quickFacts.duration: Progressive without treatment; curable with surgery

facts: Carpal tunnel syndrome affects 3-6% of the general adult population
Women are 3 times more likely to develop carpal tunnel than men
Carpal tunnel release is one of the most common hand surgeries
Wrist splinting at night relieves symptoms in 80% of mild cases
Carpal tunnel during pregnancy often resolves after delivery
Ergonomic keyboards have not been proven to prevent carpal tunnel
Surgery has a success rate of over 90%
Vitamin B6 may help mild cases though evidence is limited

prevention: Take frequent breaks during repetitive hand tasks
Keep wrists in a neutral position when typing
Use ergonomic tools and workspace setup
Stretch hands and wrists regularly
Reduce force and relax grip when working
management: Wear a wrist splint at night
Take anti-inflammatory medications for pain
Apply ice to reduce swelling
Try corticosteroid injections for inflammation
Do nerve gliding exercises
Consider surgery if conservative treatment fails after 6 months
emergency: See a doctor promptly if you notice muscle wasting in the thumb
Seek care if numbness and weakness are progressive and constant

name: Depression
overview: Depression is a mood disorder that causes persistent feelings of sadness, hopelessness, and loss of interest in activities once enjoyed. It affects how you feel, think, and handle daily activities. More than just a bout of the blues, depression is a serious medical condition that often requires long-term treatment with therapy, medication, or both.

symptoms.name: Sleep disturbances (too much or too little)

quickFacts.deathRank: Leading cause of disability worldwide
quickFacts.ageRange: Average onset in mid-20s, can occur at any age
quickFacts.preventable: Partially - early intervention and support help
quickFacts.duration: Chronic - episodes can recur, manageable with treatment

facts: Depression affects 280 million people worldwide
It is the leading cause of disability globally
Only about 50% of people with depression receive treatment
Antidepressants take 4-6 weeks to reach full effectiveness
Exercise is as effective as antidepressants for mild to moderate depression
Depression has a recurrence rate of about 50% after the first episode
The gut-brain axis plays a significant role in depression
Seasonal affective disorder (SAD) is a type of depression linked to reduced sunlight

prevention: Build and maintain strong social connections
Exercise regularly - aim for 30 minutes most days
Practice stress management and mindfulness
Get 7-9 hours of quality sleep
Limit alcohol consumption
Seek help early when feeling persistently low
management: Take antidepressant medication as prescribed
Attend regular psychotherapy (CBT, IPT)
Maintain daily routines and structured activities
Set small, achievable daily goals
Stay connected with supportive people
Avoid making major life decisions during depressive episodes
emergency: Call a crisis hotline immediately if having suicidal thoughts
Go to the nearest ER if feeling unsafe
Never leave a suicidal person alone - stay with them and call for help

name: ADHD
overview: ADHD is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by persistent patterns of inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity that interfere with daily functioning. It is one of the most common childhood disorders and frequently persists into adulthood. ADHD has three presentations: predominantly inattentive, predominantly hyperactive-impulsive, and combined.
atRisk: Premature birth or low birth weight
Prenatal exposure to tobacco or alcohol

quickFacts.deathRank: Not fatal; significantly impacts quality of life
quickFacts.ageRange: Usually diagnosed by age 12, persists in 60% of adults
quickFacts.preventable: No - neurodevelopmental condition

facts: ADHD affects 5-7% of children and 2.5-4% of adults globally
60% of children with ADHD continue to have symptoms as adults
Girls with ADHD are often underdiagnosed because they tend to be inattentive rather than hyperactive
Stimulant medications are effective in 70-80% of ADHD patients
ADHD brains have differences in dopamine regulation
Regular exercise significantly improves ADHD symptoms
ADHD is associated with higher rates of creativity and entrepreneurship
Adults with undiagnosed ADHD have higher rates of divorce, job loss, and accidents

prevention: No known prevention, as ADHD is neurodevelopmental
Avoid tobacco and alcohol during pregnancy
Reduce lead exposure in early childhood
Early screening and intervention improve outcomes
management: Take stimulant or non-stimulant medications as prescribed
Use organizational tools: planners, apps, reminders
Break large tasks into smaller, manageable steps
Create structured routines and environments
Exercise regularly to improve focus and mood
Consider cognitive behavioral therapy for adults
emergency: Seek help if ADHD is causing severe impairment at work or school
Consult a doctor if medication side effects are concerning

name: PTSD
overview: PTSD is a mental health condition triggered by experiencing or witnessing a terrifying event. It causes intense, disturbing thoughts and feelings related to the traumatic experience that persist long after the event has ended. Symptoms include flashbacks, nightmares, severe anxiety, and uncontrollable thoughts about the event.
atRisk: Survivors of physical or sexual assault

quickFacts.deathRank: Not directly fatal; high suicide risk
quickFacts.ageRange: Can occur at any age after trauma
quickFacts.preventable: Partially - early intervention after trauma helps
quickFacts.duration: Chronic - can improve significantly with treatment

facts: About 6% of the US population will experience PTSD at some point
PTSD can develop at any age, including childhood
EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing) is highly effective
Not everyone who experiences trauma develops PTSD
PTSD physically changes the brain, shrinking the hippocampus
Veterans are 2-3 times more likely to develop PTSD than civilians
Early psychological intervention after trauma can prevent PTSD
Service animals can significantly help PTSD management

prevention: Seek immediate support after traumatic events
Talk to a mental health professional early
Maintain strong social support networks
Practice resilience-building skills before high-risk situations
management: Engage in trauma-focused therapy (CPT, PE, or EMDR)
Take prescribed medications consistently
Practice grounding techniques during flashbacks
Join a PTSD support group
Avoid alcohol and drug self-medication
Establish safe, predictable daily routines
emergency: Call a crisis hotline if experiencing suicidal thoughts
Go to ER if feeling unsafe or in danger of harming yourself
Reach out to the Veterans Crisis Line at 988 (press 1)

name: Insomnia
overview: Insomnia is a sleep disorder characterized by difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep, or getting restorative sleep despite adequate opportunity. Chronic insomnia (lasting 3+ months) affects daily functioning, mood, and health. It is often linked to stress, anxiety, depression, and poor sleep habits, and increases the risk of heart disease, obesity, and mental health disorders.
atRisk: Women (more common due to hormonal changes)

symptoms.name: Waking up frequently during the night
Impaired performance at work or school

quickFacts.affectedGlobally: 30% of adults have short-term insomnia, 10% chronic
quickFacts.deathRank: Not directly fatal but increases health risks
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - through sleep hygiene practices
quickFacts.duration: Variable - can be short-term or chronic

facts: About 30% of adults report short-term insomnia symptoms
Chronic insomnia affects approximately 10% of the population
CBT-I (Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia) is the first-line treatment
The blue light from screens suppresses melatonin production
Insomnia increases the risk of depression by 10 times
Sleep restriction therapy paradoxically improves insomnia
The 4-7-8 breathing technique can help induce sleep
Insomnia costs US employers $63 billion annually in lost productivity

prevention: Maintain a consistent sleep-wake schedule
Create a dark, quiet, and cool sleep environment
Avoid screens for 1 hour before bed
Limit caffeine after 2 PM
Exercise regularly but not close to bedtime
Avoid heavy meals and alcohol before bed
management: Try Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I)
Practice relaxation techniques before bed
Use the bed only for sleep and intimacy
If unable to sleep after 20 minutes, get up and do something calming
Consider melatonin supplements for short-term use
Discuss sleep medications with your doctor for short-term relief
emergency: See a doctor if insomnia persists for more than 3 months
Seek help if sleep deprivation is affecting your ability to function safely

name: Burnout
overview: Burnout is a state of chronic physical and emotional exhaustion caused by prolonged exposure to demanding work or life situations. Recognized by the WHO as an occupational phenomenon, it involves three dimensions: exhaustion, cynicism, and reduced professional efficacy. Burnout can lead to depression, anxiety, and physical health problems if not addressed.
atRisk: Those lacking social support at work

quickFacts.affectedGlobally: 67% of workers experience burnout at some point
quickFacts.deathRank: Not directly fatal but increases disease risk
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - through boundary setting and support

facts: 67% of all workers report experiencing burnout at some point
WHO officially recognized burnout in ICD-11 as an occupational phenomenon
Burnout can lead to a 3.5x increased risk of type 2 diabetes
Healthcare workers have burnout rates exceeding 50%
Burnout is associated with a 20% increased risk of atrial fibrillation
The economic cost of workplace burnout is estimated at $125-190 billion in healthcare
Recovery from burnout typically requires 3-12 months of intentional change
Setting boundaries is the single most effective burnout prevention strategy

prevention: Set clear work-life boundaries
Take regular breaks during work
Learn to delegate and say no
Maintain hobbies and social connections outside work
Practice mindfulness and stress management
Use all your vacation days
management: Talk to your employer about workload adjustments
Seek professional counseling or therapy
Prioritize sleep, exercise, and nutrition
Reconnect with activities that bring joy
Consider a temporary leave of absence if severe
Build a support network of trusted colleagues
emergency: Seek immediate help if experiencing suicidal thoughts or severe depression
Talk to a healthcare provider if burnout is causing physical symptoms

name: Back Pain
overview: Back pain is one of the most common medical problems, affecting 80% of people at some point in their lives. It can range from a dull constant ache to a sudden sharp pain. Most back pain is mechanical in nature, caused by muscle strains, poor posture, disc problems, or degenerative changes. While usually not serious, chronic back pain is a leading cause of disability worldwide.

symptoms.name: Pain worsening with bending or lifting
Loss of bladder or bowel control

quickFacts.affectedGlobally: 619 million people at any given time
quickFacts.deathRank: #1 cause of disability globally
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - through exercise and posture correction
quickFacts.duration: Variable - most cases resolve in 4-6 weeks

facts: Low back pain is the single leading cause of disability worldwide
619 million people experience back pain at any given time
80% of adults will experience back pain at some point
Imaging (X-ray, MRI) is usually not needed for acute back pain
Staying active recovers faster than bed rest
Core strengthening exercises can prevent recurrence by 35%
Psychological factors strongly influence chronic back pain outcomes
Only 1-5% of back pain cases require surgery

prevention: Maintain good posture when sitting and standing
Strengthen core muscles with regular exercise
Use proper lifting technique
Take breaks from prolonged sitting every 30-60 minutes
Maintain a healthy weight
Sleep on a supportive mattress
management: Stay active and avoid prolonged bed rest
Apply ice for first 48 hours, then heat therapy
Take anti-inflammatory medications short-term
Try physical therapy for targeted exercises
Practice yoga or Pilates for flexibility
Consider cognitive behavioral therapy for chronic pain
emergency: Seek immediate care for loss of bladder or bowel control
Go to ER for back pain after a fall or injury with numbness
See a doctor if pain is severe and doesn't improve in 2 weeks

name: Osteoarthritis
overview: Osteoarthritis is the most common form of arthritis, occurring when the protective cartilage that cushions the ends of bones wears down over time. It most commonly affects joints in the hands, knees, hips, and spine. While damage to joints can't be reversed, symptoms can be managed through exercise, weight management, and medication.

symptoms.name: Joint pain during or after movement

quickFacts.deathRank: Not fatal but #1 cause of mobility disability
quickFacts.preventable: Partially - weight management and injury prevention help

facts: Osteoarthritis affects 528 million people worldwide
It is the most common joint disease globally
Knee OA is the 11th highest contributor to global disability
Every pound of weight loss removes 4 pounds of pressure on knees
Exercise is the most effective non-drug treatment for OA
Joint replacement surgery has a 95% satisfaction rate
Glucosamine and chondroitin have limited evidence for OA
OA has increased by 48% since 1990

prevention: Maintain a healthy weight to reduce joint stress
Exercise regularly to strengthen muscles around joints
Protect joints during sports and activities
Avoid repetitive motions that stress joints
Treat injuries promptly to prevent future OA
management: Stay active with low-impact exercise like swimming or cycling
Take pain medications as recommended by your doctor
Apply hot and cold therapy for pain relief
Use assistive devices if needed
Try physical therapy for joint-specific exercises
Consider joint replacement surgery for severe cases
emergency: See a doctor if joint pain suddenly worsens
Seek care if a joint becomes hot, red, and extremely swollen

name: Osteoporosis
overview: Osteoporosis is a condition where bones become weak and brittle, so fragile that even mild stresses like bending over or coughing can cause a fracture. It occurs when the creation of new bone doesn't keep up with the loss of old bone. Osteoporosis-related fractures most commonly occur in the hip, wrist, or spine and can be life-threatening in older adults.
atRisk: Low calcium or vitamin D intake

quickFacts.deathRank: Hip fractures have 20% mortality rate within 1 year
quickFacts.ageRange: Most common in women over 50 and men over 70
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - calcium, vitamin D, and exercise

facts: 1 in 3 women and 1 in 5 men over 50 will experience an osteoporotic fracture
Osteoporosis causes 8.9 million fractures annually worldwide
Hip fractures have a 20% mortality rate within the first year
Weight-bearing exercise increases bone density at any age
Peak bone mass is reached by age 30
A DEXA scan can detect osteoporosis before fractures occur
Calcium needs increase to 1,200 mg/day after age 50
Astronauts lose bone density in space similar to osteoporosis

prevention: Get adequate calcium (1,000-1,200 mg daily) and vitamin D
Do weight-bearing exercises like walking, dancing, or resistance training
Avoid smoking and limit alcohol
Get a DEXA bone density scan after menopause or age 65
Build strong bones in youth through exercise and nutrition
management: Take prescribed bisphosphonates or other bone medications
Supplement with calcium and vitamin D
Do balance exercises to prevent falls
Remove fall hazards at home
Wear supportive shoes
Get regular bone density monitoring
emergency: Seek immediate care after any fall if you have osteoporosis
Go to ER for hip pain or inability to bear weight after a fall

name: Knee Pain
overview: Knee pain is an extremely common complaint that can result from injury, arthritis, mechanical problems, or overuse. The knee is the largest and most complex joint in the body, making it vulnerable to various conditions. Treatment varies widely depending on the cause, from rest and physical therapy to surgery for severe cases.
atRisk: Workers who kneel or squat frequently

symptoms.name: Pain when bending or straightening knee

quickFacts.deathRank: Not fatal; major cause of mobility limitation
quickFacts.preventable: Partially - through weight management and exercise

facts: About 25% of adults experience knee pain at some point
The knee bears 1.5 times your body weight when walking
ACL tears are 4-6 times more common in female athletes
Losing 10 pounds removes 40 pounds of pressure on knees
Over 600,000 knee replacement surgeries are done annually in the US
Strengthening quadriceps and hamstrings protects the knee
Ice is more effective than heat for acute knee injuries
Many meniscus tears heal without surgery with physical therapy

prevention: Maintain a healthy weight
Strengthen leg muscles with targeted exercises
Warm up before physical activity
Wear proper footwear for sports
Avoid sudden increases in exercise intensity
Use knee pads for kneeling jobs
management: Follow RICE: Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation
Take anti-inflammatory medications for pain
Do physical therapy exercises to strengthen supporting muscles
Use a knee brace or support if needed
Try low-impact exercises like swimming
Consider corticosteroid or hyaluronic acid injections
emergency: See a doctor if knee buckles or locks
Seek immediate care if knee is severely swollen after an injury
Go to ER if unable to bear weight on the knee at all

name: Gout
overview: Gout is an inflammatory form of arthritis caused by excess uric acid in the blood crystallizing in joints, most commonly the big toe. Attacks come on suddenly, often at night, causing intense pain, swelling, redness, and tenderness. It is one of the most painful forms of arthritis but is highly treatable with medication and dietary changes.
atRisk: Diet high in purines (red meat, organ meats, seafood)

symptoms.name: Sudden intense joint pain (often big toe)
Pain often wakes you at night

quickFacts.deathRank: Not fatal but extremely painful and debilitating
quickFacts.ageRange: Most common in men 30-50 and postmenopausal women
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - through diet, hydration, and medication
quickFacts.duration: Chronic - attacks last 3-10 days; lifelong management

facts: Gout was historically called the 'disease of kings' due to rich diet association
Gout attacks often start in the middle of the night
Cherry juice may reduce gout flares by 35%
Allopurinol can prevent gout attacks when taken daily
Beer increases gout risk more than other alcoholic beverages
Gout has increased by 50% in recent decades linked to obesity
Uric acid levels above 6.8 mg/dL can trigger crystal formation
Colchicine, derived from autumn crocus, has been used to treat gout since ancient times

prevention: Limit purine-rich foods: red meat, organ meats, shellfish
Reduce alcohol consumption, especially beer
Stay well-hydrated with water
Maintain a healthy weight gradually
Avoid sugary drinks with high-fructose corn syrup
Take urate-lowering therapy if prescribed
management: Take anti-inflammatory medications at first sign of attack
Apply ice to the affected joint for 20 minutes
Elevate the affected joint
Take allopurinol or febuxostat to lower uric acid long-term
Eat cherries or take cherry extract
Monitor uric acid levels regularly
emergency: See a doctor if joint pain is accompanied by fever
Seek care for a first-time gout attack for proper diagnosis

name: Acne
overview: Acne is a skin condition that occurs when hair follicles become clogged with oil and dead skin cells, leading to whiteheads, blackheads, pimples, or cysts. It most commonly affects teenagers and young adults but can occur at any age. While not dangerous, severe acne can cause emotional distress and permanent scarring if not properly treated.

quickFacts.deathRank: Not fatal; significant psychological impact
quickFacts.ageRange: Most common ages 12-24, can persist into 40s
quickFacts.preventable: Partially - good skincare helps reduce severity
quickFacts.duration: Variable - months to years; most resolve by 30s

facts: Acne is the 8th most prevalent disease globally
85% of teenagers experience some form of acne
Isotretinoin (Accutane) cures severe acne in 85% of patients
Acne has no proven link to eating chocolate or greasy foods
Picking or squeezing pimples increases scarring risk
Adult acne is increasing, especially in women
Stress hormones (cortisol) can worsen acne
Retinoids are the gold standard topical treatment for acne

prevention: Wash face twice daily with a gentle cleanser
Don't touch your face with unwashed hands
Use non-comedogenic skincare and makeup products
Shower after sweating
Eat a balanced diet and limit high-glycemic foods
management: Use benzoyl peroxide or salicylic acid products
Apply retinoids for persistent acne
See a dermatologist for severe or scarring acne
Take prescribed antibiotics or hormonal treatments if recommended
Be patient - treatments take 6-8 weeks to show results
Avoid picking, squeezing, or popping pimples
emergency: See a dermatologist for sudden severe cystic acne
Seek care if acne is causing significant emotional distress

name: Eczema
overview: Eczema (atopic dermatitis) is a chronic inflammatory skin condition that causes itchy, red, dry, and cracked skin. It often begins in childhood and can flare and remit throughout life. Eczema is closely linked to an overactive immune response and is part of the 'atopic triad' along with asthma and allergic rhinitis.
atRisk: Family history of eczema, asthma, or allergies

quickFacts.deathRank: Not fatal but severely impacts quality of life
quickFacts.ageRange: Usually starts before age 5, can persist into adulthood
quickFacts.preventable: No - but flares can be prevented
quickFacts.duration: Chronic - lifelong with periods of remission

facts: Eczema affects up to 20% of children and 3% of adults globally
The 'itch-scratch cycle' is the biggest challenge in eczema management
Moisturizing within 3 minutes of bathing ('soak and seal') is critical
Dupilumab is a biologic drug that has transformed severe eczema treatment
60% of children with eczema outgrow it by adulthood
Bleach baths can reduce eczema flares by controlling skin bacteria
Eczema patients have a deficiency in the filaggrin protein in their skin
Stress is one of the most common eczema triggers

prevention: Moisturize skin daily, especially after bathing
Use fragrance-free soaps and detergents
Avoid known triggers (certain fabrics, foods, stress)
Keep indoor humidity between 30-50%
Wear soft, breathable fabrics like cotton
management: Apply prescribed topical corticosteroids during flares
Moisturize at least twice daily with thick emollients
Take lukewarm (not hot) baths and pat skin dry
Try wet wrap therapy for severe flares
Keep nails short to minimize scratching damage
Consider immunomodulator medications for severe cases
emergency: Seek care for signs of skin infection: increased redness, warmth, pus
See a doctor if eczema is severely affecting sleep or daily life

name: Psoriasis
overview: Psoriasis is a chronic autoimmune condition that causes rapid buildup of skin cells, resulting in thick, red, scaly patches that can be itchy and painful. The most common type, plaque psoriasis, appears as raised, inflamed patches covered with silvery-white scales. Psoriasis is associated with psoriatic arthritis and increased cardiovascular disease risk.

symptoms.name: Dry, cracked skin that may bleed

quickFacts.deathRank: Not directly fatal; associated with cardiovascular risk
quickFacts.preventable: No - autoimmune condition
quickFacts.duration: Chronic - lifelong with flares and remissions

facts: Psoriasis affects 2-3% of the global population
30% of psoriasis patients develop psoriatic arthritis
Skin cells grow 10 times faster in psoriasis than normal
Biologic drugs have achieved 90% skin clearance in some patients
Psoriasis increases heart attack risk by 28%
Stress is the most commonly reported trigger for flares
Psoriasis is not contagious
UV light therapy (phototherapy) is effective for many psoriasis patients

prevention: Cannot prevent psoriasis, but manage triggers
Avoid excessive alcohol and smoking
Manage stress with relaxation techniques
Moisturize skin regularly
Get moderate sun exposure (avoid sunburn)
management: Use prescribed topical treatments (corticosteroids, vitamin D analogs)
Try phototherapy (UV light treatment)
Consider biologic medications for moderate to severe cases
Keep skin moisturized to reduce scaling
Avoid skin injuries that can trigger new patches (Koebner phenomenon)
Monitor for signs of psoriatic arthritis
emergency: Seek care for erythrodermic psoriasis (widespread redness covering most of body)
See a doctor for joint pain or stiffness suggesting psoriatic arthritis

name: Kidney Stones
overview: Kidney stones are hard deposits of minerals and salts that form inside the kidneys. They can cause excruciating pain when passing through the urinary tract. Most small stones pass on their own with fluids and pain management, while larger stones may require medical procedures like lithotripsy or surgery. Recurrence is common without preventive measures.
atRisk: Family or personal history of kidney stones

symptoms.name: Severe pain in side and back below ribs
Pain radiating to lower abdomen and groin

quickFacts.affectedGlobally: Affects 1 in 10 people in their lifetime
quickFacts.deathRank: Rarely fatal; one of the most painful conditions
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - drinking enough water is key
quickFacts.duration: Acute - most stones pass in 1-3 weeks

facts: 1 in 10 people will have a kidney stone in their lifetime
Drinking 2.5 liters of water daily reduces kidney stone risk by 50%
Calcium oxalate stones are the most common type (80%)
The pain of kidney stones is often compared to childbirth
Lemonade can help prevent stones due to citric acid content
Kidney stone recurrence rate is 50% within 5-10 years
Climate change is expected to increase kidney stone rates due to heat
Roller coasters have been shown to help pass small kidney stones

prevention: Drink at least 2.5-3 liters of water daily
Reduce sodium intake to under 2,300 mg per day
Eat adequate calcium from food (not supplements)
Limit animal protein and oxalate-rich foods
Drink lemon water regularly
Maintain a healthy weight
management: Drink plenty of fluids to help pass small stones
Take prescribed pain medications
Strain urine to collect stones for analysis
Take alpha-blockers to help pass stones faster
Consider lithotripsy for stones larger than 5mm
Follow dietary recommendations based on stone type
emergency: Seek immediate care for fever with kidney stone (possible infection)
Go to ER if unable to urinate or pain is unbearable
Call emergency for blood in urine with severe pain

name: Urinary Tract Infection
overview: A urinary tract infection is a bacterial infection in any part of the urinary system, including the kidneys, bladder, ureters, or urethra. Most UTIs involve the lower urinary tract (bladder and urethra). Women are at significantly greater risk due to shorter urethras. While most UTIs are easily treated with antibiotics, untreated infections can spread to the kidneys and become serious.
atRisk: Women (50x more common than in men)

quickFacts.deathRank: Rarely fatal but kidney infection can be dangerous
quickFacts.ageRange: All ages; most common in women 18-50
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - through hygiene and hydration
quickFacts.duration: Acute - resolves in 3-7 days with antibiotics

facts: 50-60% of women will experience at least one UTI in their lifetime
UTIs account for 150 million infections globally each year
Cranberry products may help prevent UTIs but don't treat them
E. coli bacteria causes 80-90% of UTIs
Antibiotic resistance in UTI-causing bacteria is increasing worldwide
Post-menopausal women are more susceptible due to decreased estrogen
Drinking water after sexual intercourse helps prevent UTIs
D-mannose supplement may help prevent recurrent UTIs

prevention: Drink plenty of water daily
Urinate after sexual intercourse
Wipe from front to back after using the toilet
Avoid irritating feminine products
Don't hold urine for prolonged periods
Consider cranberry supplements for recurrent UTIs
management: Complete the full course of prescribed antibiotics
Drink plenty of fluids to flush bacteria
Take pain relief medication as needed
Use a heating pad for pelvic discomfort
Avoid caffeine, alcohol, and spicy foods during infection
emergency: Seek immediate care for back pain, fever, or chills (kidney infection)
Go to ER if unable to keep fluids down with UTI symptoms
See a doctor if UTI symptoms don't improve within 2 days of antibiotics

name: Chronic Kidney Disease
overview: Chronic kidney disease is a progressive loss of kidney function over months or years. The kidneys filter waste and excess fluids from the blood, and when they fail, dangerous levels of fluid and waste build up in the body. Diabetes and high blood pressure are the leading causes. CKD often has no symptoms until advanced stages and can eventually require dialysis or transplant.

symptoms.name: Usually no symptoms in early stages

quickFacts.deathRank: #12 cause of death globally
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - managing diabetes and blood pressure

facts: 850 million people worldwide are affected by kidney disease
CKD is the 12th leading cause of death globally
1 in 10 adults has some form of chronic kidney disease
90% of people with CKD don't know they have it
Diabetes causes 30-50% of all CKD cases
A simple blood and urine test can detect CKD
Kidney transplant recipients can live 15-20+ years with a transplant
Dialysis costs approximately $90,000 per patient per year in the US

prevention: Control blood sugar if diabetic
Manage blood pressure below 130/80 mmHg
Avoid overuse of NSAIDs and painkillers
Stay hydrated with adequate water intake
Get annual kidney function tests if at risk
Quit smoking
management: Follow a kidney-friendly diet (low sodium, potassium, phosphorus)
Take all prescribed medications for blood pressure and diabetes
Monitor kidney function with regular blood tests (GFR, creatinine)
Limit protein intake as advised by your doctor
Prepare for dialysis or transplant if disease progresses
Manage anemia with prescribed medications
emergency: Seek immediate care for severe swelling, shortness of breath, or confusion
Go to ER if urine output drops dramatically or stops

name: Allergies
overview: Allergies occur when the immune system overreacts to a normally harmless substance (allergen) such as pollen, dust mites, pet dander, or certain foods. The body produces antibodies that trigger inflammation in the sinuses, airways, skin, or digestive system. While most allergies are mild, severe allergic reactions (anaphylaxis) can be life-threatening.
atRisk: Family history of allergies or asthma

symptoms.name: Swelling of face, lips, or tongue
Anaphylaxis: throat closing, drop in blood pressure

quickFacts.affectedGlobally: Over 400 million people with allergic rhinitis
quickFacts.deathRank: Anaphylaxis causes 1,500 deaths/year in the US
quickFacts.ageRange: Can develop at any age, often in childhood
quickFacts.preventable: No - but reactions are manageable

facts: Allergies are the 6th leading cause of chronic illness in the US
Food allergies affect 8% of children and 4% of adults
Allergen immunotherapy (allergy shots) can reduce symptoms by 85%
The hygiene hypothesis suggests too-clean environments increase allergy risk
Peanut allergies have doubled in Western countries over the past 20 years
Allergic rhinitis affects 10-30% of the global population
Antihistamines block histamine, the chemical causing allergy symptoms
