Early introduction of peanuts to infants can reduce peanut allergy risk by 81%

prevention: Identify allergens through testing
Use HEPA air filters in your home
Keep windows closed during high pollen seasons
Shower after spending time outdoors
Use allergen-proof covers on bedding
Introduce common allergens to infants early (under medical guidance)
management: Take antihistamines for symptom relief
Use nasal corticosteroid sprays for congestion
Consider allergen immunotherapy for long-term relief
Carry an epinephrine auto-injector if at risk for anaphylaxis
Avoid known allergens when possible
Use saline nasal rinses to clear allergens
emergency: Use epinephrine (EpiPen) immediately for anaphylaxis
Call emergency services even after using an EpiPen
Lie down and elevate legs if experiencing anaphylaxis

name: Obesity
overview: Obesity is a complex, chronic disease defined by excessive body fat accumulation (BMI of 30 or higher) that impairs health. It results from a combination of genetic, behavioral, environmental, and metabolic factors. Obesity significantly increases the risk of heart disease, diabetes, certain cancers, and premature death, and is now recognized as the leading preventable cause of death after smoking.

quickFacts.deathRank: Contributes to 2.8 million deaths annually
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - through diet, exercise, and lifestyle

facts: Over 1 billion people worldwide are obese according to WHO
Childhood obesity has increased 10-fold in the past 40 years
Obesity increases cancer risk for at least 13 types of cancer
Bariatric surgery can lead to 50-70% excess weight loss
GLP-1 receptor agonists are a new class of highly effective weight loss drugs
Sleep deprivation increases hunger hormones and promotes weight gain
Obesity costs global healthcare systems over $2 trillion annually
Gut microbiome composition differs between obese and lean individuals

prevention: Eat a balanced diet with appropriate portions
Exercise at least 150-300 minutes per week
Limit processed foods, sugary drinks, and fast food
Get 7-9 hours of sleep nightly
Manage stress without using food as comfort
Monitor weight regularly
management: Set realistic weight loss goals (1-2 pounds per week)
Work with a dietitian for a personalized meal plan
Increase physical activity gradually
Consider FDA-approved weight loss medications
Discuss bariatric surgery for BMI 40+ or 35+ with comorbidities
Join a support group or weight management program
emergency: Seek care for chest pain, severe shortness of breath, or leg swelling
Consult a doctor for rapid unexplained weight gain

name: Anemia
overview: Anemia is a condition where the blood doesn't have enough healthy red blood cells or hemoglobin to carry adequate oxygen to tissues. Iron deficiency is the most common cause, but it can also result from vitamin B12 or folate deficiency, chronic disease, or genetic conditions. Symptoms include fatigue, weakness, and pale skin, ranging from mild to life-threatening.

quickFacts.deathRank: Contributes to 68,000 maternal deaths annually
quickFacts.ageRange: All ages; most common in women and children
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - iron and folate-rich diet prevents most cases

facts: Anemia affects nearly 1.8 billion people globally
Iron deficiency is the most common nutritional deficiency worldwide
Vitamin C increases iron absorption by up to 6 times
Tea and coffee can inhibit iron absorption by up to 60%
Anemia in pregnancy doubles the risk of preterm birth
Sickle cell anemia is the most common genetic blood disorder
Cooking in iron pots can increase the iron content of food
B12 deficiency anemia is common in strict vegans

prevention: Eat iron-rich foods: red meat, spinach, beans, fortified cereals
Pair iron-rich foods with vitamin C for better absorption
Take prenatal vitamins during pregnancy
Avoid tea or coffee with iron-rich meals
Screen regularly if at risk
Supplement with B12 if following a vegan diet
management: Take prescribed iron supplements on an empty stomach
Get B12 injections if deficiency is severe
Treat underlying causes (heavy menstruation, GI bleeding)
Monitor hemoglobin levels regularly
Eat a balanced diet rich in iron, folate, and B12
Consider iron infusions for severe cases
emergency: Seek care for severe fatigue with rapid heartbeat or chest pain
Go to ER for fainting or extremely pale skin

name: Vitamin D Deficiency
overview: Vitamin D deficiency occurs when the body doesn't get or make enough vitamin D, a hormone essential for calcium absorption, bone health, immune function, and mood regulation. It is one of the most common nutritional deficiencies worldwide, partly because modern lifestyles involve limited sun exposure. Severe deficiency can cause rickets in children and osteomalacia in adults.

quickFacts.deathRank: Not directly fatal but increases disease risk
quickFacts.ageRange: All ages; most common in elderly and office workers
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - sun exposure and supplementation

facts: An estimated 1 billion people worldwide have vitamin D deficiency
10-15 minutes of midday sun exposure can produce 10,000-20,000 IU of vitamin D
Vitamin D deficiency is linked to increased risk of 17 types of cancer
50% of the global population has insufficient vitamin D levels
Vitamin D is actually a hormone, not a traditional vitamin
Darker skin needs 3-6 times more sun exposure to produce the same vitamin D
Vitamin D deficiency is linked to increased depression and anxiety
Most foods are naturally low in vitamin D except fatty fish and egg yolks

prevention: Get 10-30 minutes of midday sunlight several times a week
Eat vitamin D-rich foods: fatty fish, egg yolks, fortified milk
Take vitamin D supplements (1,000-2,000 IU daily for most adults)
Get tested if you have risk factors
Consider higher doses in winter months or northern climates
management: Take prescribed high-dose vitamin D supplements
Get blood levels checked every 3-6 months during treatment
Take vitamin D with a fat-containing meal for better absorption
Aim for blood levels of 30-50 ng/mL
Combine with adequate calcium intake
Address underlying absorption issues if present
emergency: See a doctor for severe bone pain or unexplained fractures
Seek care if symptoms worsen despite supplementation

name: Myopia
overview: Myopia (nearsightedness) is a common vision condition where close objects appear clear but distant objects appear blurry. It occurs when the eyeball is too long or the cornea curves too steeply, causing light to focus in front of the retina instead of on it. Myopia is increasing rapidly worldwide, particularly in East Asia, and is projected to affect half the global population by 2050.

symptoms.name: Need to sit closer to screens

quickFacts.deathRank: Not fatal; leading cause of correctable vision impairment
quickFacts.preventable: Partially - outdoor time reduces childhood onset
quickFacts.duration: Chronic - correctable with lenses or surgery

facts: Myopia affects 2.6 billion people globally and is increasing rapidly
By 2050, an estimated 5 billion people (half the world) will be myopic
Spending 2+ hours outdoors daily reduces myopia risk in children by 50%
LASIK surgery permanently reshapes the cornea with 96% patient satisfaction
High myopia (over -6 diopters) increases risk of retinal detachment and glaucoma
In South Korea and China, over 80% of young adults are myopic
Atropine eye drops can slow myopia progression in children by 50-60%
The 20-20-20 rule: every 20 minutes, look 20 feet away for 20 seconds

prevention: Encourage children to spend 2+ hours outdoors daily
Follow the 20-20-20 rule for screen time
Ensure proper lighting when reading
Hold books and screens at appropriate distances
Limit continuous near-work to 30-minute intervals
Get annual eye exams for children
management: Wear prescribed glasses or contact lenses
Consider atropine drops for myopia control in children
Use orthokeratology lenses if recommended
Discuss LASIK or PRK surgery for adults with stable prescription
Get regular eye exams to monitor progression
Protect eyes from UV rays with sunglasses
emergency: Seek immediate care for sudden flashes, floaters, or curtain-like vision loss (retinal detachment)
See an ophthalmologist urgently for sudden vision changes

name: Glaucoma
overview: Glaucoma is a group of eye conditions that damage the optic nerve, usually due to abnormally high pressure in the eye. It is the leading cause of irreversible blindness worldwide. Open-angle glaucoma, the most common form, develops slowly with no early symptoms. Regular eye exams are the only way to detect it early, making screening critical for at-risk populations.

quickFacts.deathRank: #1 cause of irreversible blindness
quickFacts.preventable: No - but vision loss is preventable with early treatment

facts: Glaucoma affects 80 million people and is the leading cause of irreversible blindness
50% of people with glaucoma don't know they have it
Vision lost to glaucoma cannot be recovered
A comprehensive eye exam every 1-2 years can detect glaucoma early
African Americans are 6-8 times more likely to develop glaucoma
Eye drops can reduce eye pressure by 20-30%
Marijuana has been proposed but is not an effective long-term treatment
New MIGS (minimally invasive glaucoma surgeries) are transforming treatment

prevention: Get comprehensive eye exams every 1-2 years after age 40
Know your family history of glaucoma
Exercise regularly to lower eye pressure
Protect eyes from injury
Use prescribed eye drops consistently
management: Use prescribed eye drops daily without missing doses
Attend regular follow-up appointments to monitor pressure
Consider laser treatment or surgery if drops aren't sufficient
Avoid head-down positions for prolonged periods
Be aware of medication interactions that affect eye pressure
Use low-vision aids if vision loss has occurred
emergency: Seek immediate care for sudden eye pain, blurred vision, and halos (angle-closure glaucoma)
Go to ER for acute angle-closure: it requires emergency treatment to prevent blindness

name: Dry Eye Syndrome
overview: Dry eye syndrome occurs when tears don't provide adequate lubrication for the eyes, either due to insufficient tear production or poor-quality tears that evaporate too quickly. It is increasingly common due to screen use and is one of the most frequent reasons for eye doctor visits. While rarely serious, chronic dry eye can cause eye damage and significantly impact quality of life.
atRisk: Screen users (computer, phone) for extended periods

quickFacts.deathRank: Not fatal; significant impact on daily comfort
quickFacts.ageRange: Increases after age 50; rising in younger adults due to screens
quickFacts.preventable: Partially - through screen habits and environment

facts: Dry eye affects an estimated 5-50% of the global population
We blink 66% less when using digital screens
The 20-20-20 rule significantly reduces digital eye strain
Omega-3 fatty acids can improve tear quality
Dry eye is the most common reason for eye doctor visits
Air conditioning and heating worsen dry eye symptoms
Preservative-free artificial tears are recommended for frequent use
Intense Pulsed Light (IPL) therapy is a newer treatment for evaporative dry eye

prevention: Follow the 20-20-20 rule when using screens
Blink consciously and frequently during screen use
Use a humidifier in dry indoor environments
Position computer screen below eye level
Wear wraparound glasses in windy conditions
Stay hydrated by drinking plenty of water
management: Use preservative-free artificial tears regularly
Apply warm compresses to eyelids daily
Take omega-3 fatty acid supplements
Use prescription eye drops (cyclosporine or lifitegrast) if needed
Consider punctal plugs to retain tears
Clean eyelids with gentle lid wipes
emergency: See an eye doctor if vision becomes significantly blurred
Seek care for severe eye pain or signs of infection

name: Tinnitus
overview: Tinnitus is the perception of sound when no external sound is present, commonly described as ringing, buzzing, hissing, or humming in the ears. It is not a disease itself but a symptom of an underlying condition such as hearing loss, ear injury, or circulatory problems. While often not serious, chronic tinnitus can significantly impact sleep, concentration, and mental health.

symptoms.name: Ringing in one or both ears
Anxiety and depression from constant noise

quickFacts.deathRank: Not fatal; major impact on mental health
quickFacts.preventable: Partially - protecting hearing prevents many cases
quickFacts.duration: Often chronic - some cases resolve spontaneously

facts: About 10-15% of the global adult population experiences tinnitus
Tinnitus is the #1 service-connected disability among US veterans
There is currently no cure for most forms of tinnitus
Sound therapy and habituation can make tinnitus less noticeable over time
Exposure to 85 dB or louder sounds for prolonged periods causes hearing damage
Cognitive behavioral therapy reduces tinnitus distress by 40%
Earwax removal sometimes resolves tinnitus immediately
Pulsatile tinnitus (hearing heartbeat) may indicate a vascular condition

prevention: Wear hearing protection at concerts and in loud environments
Keep headphone volume below 60% of maximum
Follow the 60/60 rule: 60% volume for 60 minutes maximum
Take breaks from loud noise exposure
Get regular hearing checkups
management: Use white noise or sound machines to mask tinnitus
Try cognitive behavioral therapy for tinnitus
Consider hearing aids if hearing loss is present
Practice relaxation techniques to reduce stress-related worsening
Avoid caffeine and alcohol which can worsen symptoms
Join tinnitus support groups
emergency: See a doctor immediately for sudden-onset tinnitus in one ear
Seek urgent care for pulsatile tinnitus (may indicate vascular problem)

name: Vertigo
overview: Vertigo is a sensation of spinning dizziness that feels like the room is moving around you. It is usually caused by inner ear problems, with BPPV (benign paroxysmal positional vertigo) being the most common cause. Episodes can last seconds to days and can be accompanied by nausea, vomiting, and balance problems. Most forms of vertigo are treatable with repositioning maneuvers or medication.

symptoms.name: Symptoms triggered by head position changes

quickFacts.deathRank: Not fatal; major cause of falls in elderly
quickFacts.preventable: Partially - treating underlying conditions helps
quickFacts.duration: Variable - BPPV resolves quickly with treatment

facts: BPPV accounts for about 50% of all vertigo cases
The Epley maneuver cures BPPV in 80% of patients in one session
Vertigo is a symptom, not a disease itself
Inner ear crystals (otoconia) becoming dislodged cause BPPV
Meniere's disease causes episodes of vertigo lasting hours
Vestibular rehabilitation exercises can retrain the brain for balance
Vertigo increases fall risk by 12 times in elderly patients
Some vertigo cases are caused by vestibular migraine

prevention: Move slowly when changing positions, especially getting out of bed
Treat ear infections promptly
Stay hydrated to maintain inner ear fluid balance
Manage migraine triggers if you have vestibular migraine
management: Learn and perform the Epley maneuver for BPPV
Take prescribed vestibular suppressant medications
Do vestibular rehabilitation exercises
Reduce salt intake if diagnosed with Meniere's disease
Avoid sudden head movements during episodes
Sit or lie down immediately during severe episodes
emergency: Seek immediate care for vertigo with severe headache, double vision, or weakness (possible stroke)
Call emergency if unable to walk or stand due to vertigo
See a doctor for vertigo episodes lasting more than a few hours

name: Dental Cavities
overview: Dental cavities (caries) are permanently damaged areas in the hard surface of teeth that develop into tiny holes. They are caused by bacteria that produce acid from sugars in food, gradually dissolving tooth enamel. Cavities are the most common chronic disease worldwide, affecting nearly every adult and most children. They are entirely preventable with proper oral hygiene and regular dental care.
atRisk: Frequent snacking on sugary or acidic foods

symptoms.name: Tooth sensitivity to hot, cold, or sweet
Visible holes or pits in teeth

quickFacts.deathRank: Not fatal but severe infections can spread
quickFacts.ageRange: All ages from first tooth to old age
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - through brushing, fluoride, and diet
quickFacts.duration: Progressive without treatment; treatable with fillings

facts: Tooth decay is the most common chronic disease in the world
3.5 billion people have untreated oral diseases globally
Fluoride in water reduces cavities by 25%
Sugar-free gum with xylitol can reduce cavity risk by 40%
A cavity can develop in as little as 6 months
Cheese can neutralize acid in the mouth and protect teeth
Dental sealants prevent 80% of cavities in back teeth
Saliva naturally fights cavity-causing bacteria

prevention: Brush teeth twice daily with fluoride toothpaste
Floss daily to remove plaque between teeth
Limit sugary snacks and drinks
Visit the dentist every 6 months for checkups
Consider dental sealants for children
Drink fluoridated water
management: Get fillings for small to moderate cavities
Use high-fluoride toothpaste if prone to cavities
Consider crowns for severely decayed teeth
Get root canal treatment for deep decay reaching the nerve
Chew sugar-free gum with xylitol after meals
Address dry mouth conditions with your dentist
emergency: See a dentist urgently for severe toothache or swelling
Go to ER if facial swelling from tooth infection affects breathing or swallowing

name: Gum Disease
overview: Gum disease is a common infection of the tissues surrounding and supporting the teeth. It begins as gingivitis (gum inflammation) with red, swollen gums that bleed easily, and can progress to periodontitis, where gums pull away from teeth and bone is destroyed. Gum disease is the leading cause of tooth loss in adults and is linked to heart disease, diabetes, and other systemic conditions.
atRisk: Crooked teeth that are hard to clean

quickFacts.deathRank: Not directly fatal; linked to heart disease risk
quickFacts.ageRange: Gingivitis can start in childhood; periodontitis after 30
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - proper oral hygiene and dental visits

facts: Severe gum disease affects about 19% of the global adult population
Gum disease increases heart disease risk by 20%
Smoking increases gum disease risk by 2-7 times
Gingivitis is reversible with proper oral care
Periodontal disease is linked to Alzheimer's disease risk
Flossing removes plaque from 35% of tooth surfaces unreachable by brushing
Diabetes and gum disease have a two-way relationship
Pregnant women with gum disease have a higher risk of preterm birth

prevention: Brush teeth twice daily for 2 minutes
Floss or use interdental brushes daily
Get professional dental cleanings every 6 months
Quit smoking and tobacco use
Eat a balanced diet and limit sugary foods
management: Get professional deep cleaning (scaling and root planing)
Use prescribed antibacterial mouthwash
Attend more frequent dental cleanings (every 3-4 months)
Consider gum surgery for advanced periodontitis
Manage diabetes to improve gum health
Use a soft-bristled or electric toothbrush
emergency: See a dentist promptly for loose teeth or gum abscess
Seek care for severe gum pain with fever

name: Endometriosis
overview: Endometriosis is a chronic condition where tissue similar to the uterine lining grows outside the uterus, commonly on the ovaries, fallopian tubes, and pelvic tissues. This displaced tissue continues to act as it normally would - thickening, breaking down, and bleeding with each menstrual cycle - causing severe pain, inflammation, and often fertility problems.
atRisk: Heavy periods lasting more than 7 days

symptoms.name: Pain with bowel movements or urination

quickFacts.deathRank: Not fatal; major cause of chronic pain and infertility
quickFacts.preventable: No - cause is not fully understood

facts: Endometriosis affects approximately 10% of women of reproductive age
Average diagnosis delay is 7-10 years from symptom onset
30-50% of women with endometriosis experience infertility
Laparoscopic surgery is the gold standard for diagnosis
Endometriosis costs the US economy $78 billion annually
Many women are told their pain is 'normal' before diagnosis
Hormonal treatments can slow endometriosis growth but not cure it
Endometriosis can rarely occur in locations outside the pelvis

prevention: No proven prevention exists
Hormonal contraceptives may reduce risk
Regular exercise may lower estrogen levels
Avoiding excessive alcohol consumption may help
management: Take prescribed hormonal therapy (birth control pills, GnRH agonists)
Use pain management with NSAIDs or prescribed medications
Consider laparoscopic surgery to remove endometrial tissue
Apply heat therapy for pelvic pain relief
Explore fertility treatments if trying to conceive
Join an endometriosis support group
emergency: Seek immediate care for sudden severe pelvic pain (possible cyst rupture)
See a doctor for heavy bleeding soaking through a pad every hour

name: Menopause
overview: Menopause is a natural biological process marking the end of menstrual cycles, diagnosed after 12 consecutive months without a period. It typically occurs between ages 45-55 as the ovaries produce less estrogen and progesterone. While a normal part of aging, menopause symptoms like hot flashes, mood changes, and bone loss can significantly affect quality of life and long-term health.

quickFacts.affectedGlobally: 1.5 billion women will be menopausal by 2025
quickFacts.deathRank: Not a disease; increases osteoporosis and heart disease risk
quickFacts.preventable: No - natural biological process
quickFacts.duration: Transition lasts 4-8 years; some symptoms may persist

facts: The average age of menopause worldwide is 51 years
Perimenopause can begin 4-8 years before menopause
75% of women experience hot flashes during menopause
Women lose up to 20% of bone density in the first 5-7 years after menopause
Hormone replacement therapy remains the most effective treatment for hot flashes
Heart disease risk increases significantly after menopause due to estrogen decline
Japanese women have fewer hot flashes, possibly due to soy-rich diets
Menopause can cause brain fog, but cognitive function typically returns

prevention: Cannot prevent menopause, but prepare for the transition
Build strong bones with calcium and vitamin D before menopause
Exercise regularly to maintain healthy weight and bone density
Quit smoking to avoid earlier onset
management: Discuss hormone replacement therapy (HRT) with your doctor
Dress in layers and keep rooms cool for hot flashes
Use vaginal moisturizers or estrogen for dryness
Exercise regularly including weight-bearing activities
Get adequate calcium (1,200 mg) and vitamin D
Practice mindfulness or CBT for mood symptoms
emergency: See a doctor for any bleeding after menopause (could indicate a serious condition)
Seek care for severe depression or anxiety during menopause transition

name: Dehydration
overview: Dehydration occurs when the body loses more fluids than it takes in, disrupting normal bodily functions. Even mild dehydration can cause fatigue, headaches, and impaired concentration. Severe dehydration is a medical emergency that can lead to organ failure and death. Common causes include inadequate fluid intake, excessive heat, exercise, vomiting, and diarrhea.
atRisk: Those with diarrhea or vomiting illnesses
Diabetes patients with high blood sugar

quickFacts.affectedGlobally: Contributes to 1.5 million child deaths annually from diarrheal disease
quickFacts.deathRank: Can be fatal in severe cases, especially in children and elderly
quickFacts.ageRange: All ages; most dangerous in very young and elderly
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - adequate fluid intake

facts: 75% of Americans are estimated to be chronically mildly dehydrated
Losing just 2% of body water impairs cognitive function
Oral rehydration solution (ORS) has saved over 70 million lives since 1970s
Thirst is not a reliable indicator - you're already dehydrated when thirsty
Coffee in moderate amounts does not cause significant dehydration
Dehydration is the most common cause of daytime fatigue
Dark urine color is the simplest indicator of dehydration
Elderly people have a reduced sense of thirst, increasing dehydration risk

prevention: Drink 8-10 glasses of water daily (more in heat or exercise)
Don't wait until thirsty to drink
Eat water-rich fruits and vegetables
Increase fluid intake during illness, heat, or exercise
Monitor urine color - aim for pale yellow
Avoid excessive alcohol and caffeine in hot weather
management: Sip water or oral rehydration solution slowly
Replace electrolytes with ORS or sports drinks for moderate cases
Rest in a cool environment
Avoid caffeinated and alcoholic beverages
Eat water-rich foods like watermelon and cucumbers
emergency: Seek immediate care for signs of severe dehydration: confusion, no urination, rapid heartbeat
Call emergency for children with sunken eyes, no tears, or extreme lethargy
IV fluids are needed for severe dehydration

name: Food Poisoning
overview: Food poisoning is illness caused by eating contaminated food containing infectious bacteria, viruses, parasites, or their toxins. Symptoms typically develop within hours to days after eating contaminated food and usually resolve on their own. Common culprits include Salmonella, E. coli, Norovirus, and Listeria. While usually mild, food poisoning can be life-threatening for vulnerable populations.
atRisk: People eating raw or undercooked foods
Those with chronic liver or kidney disease

quickFacts.affectedGlobally: 600 million cases per year (1 in 10 people)
quickFacts.deathRank: 420,000 deaths annually worldwide
quickFacts.ageRange: All ages; most dangerous in young and elderly
quickFacts.preventable: Yes - through food safety practices
quickFacts.duration: Acute - most cases resolve in 1-3 days

facts: 1 in 10 people gets food poisoning every year globally
Norovirus is the most common cause of foodborne illness
Raw chicken is the most common source of Salmonella
The 'danger zone' for food is between 40-140F (4-60C)
Food poisoning is responsible for 420,000 deaths annually worldwide
Proper hand washing prevents 50% of foodborne illness
Leftovers should be refrigerated within 2 hours of cooking
Most food poisoning cases are actually caused by viruses, not bacteria

prevention: Wash hands thoroughly before handling food
Cook meat to proper internal temperatures
Refrigerate perishable foods within 2 hours
Avoid cross-contamination between raw and cooked foods
Wash fruits and vegetables before eating
Don't eat food that smells or looks suspicious
management: Stay hydrated with water, broth, or oral rehydration solutions
Rest and eat bland foods (BRAT: bananas, rice, applesauce, toast)
Avoid dairy, caffeine, and fatty foods until recovered
Take anti-diarrheal medication only if no fever or bloody stool
Gradually return to normal diet as symptoms improve
emergency: Seek care for bloody diarrhea, fever above 101.5F, or inability to keep fluids down
Go to ER for signs of dehydration: no urination, dizziness, rapid heartbeat
Call a doctor for food poisoning in pregnant women, children, or elderly

name: Fibromyalgia
overview: Fibromyalgia is a chronic condition characterized by widespread musculoskeletal pain accompanied by fatigue, sleep, memory, and mood issues. It amplifies painful sensations by affecting the way the brain and spinal cord process pain signals. While not fully understood, it often develops after physical trauma, surgery, infection, or significant psychological stress.

symptoms.name: Sensitivity to temperature, light, and noise

quickFacts.deathRank: Not fatal but severely impacts quality of life
quickFacts.preventable: No - cause is not fully understood

facts: Fibromyalgia affects approximately 2-4% of the population globally
It was not recognized as a real medical condition until 1987
Women are 7 times more likely to be diagnosed than men
Fibromyalgia involves central sensitization - the nervous system amplifies pain
There is no single diagnostic test - it is diagnosed by symptoms
Low-impact exercise is one of the most effective treatments
Fibromyalgia often coexists with other conditions like IBS, migraine, and TMJ
Three FDA-approved medications exist specifically for fibromyalgia

prevention: No proven prevention, but managing stress may reduce risk
Get adequate sleep and maintain sleep hygiene
Stay physically active with regular gentle exercise
Seek early treatment for pain conditions
management: Exercise regularly - start low and go slow (walking, swimming, yoga)
Take prescribed medications (pregabalin, duloxetine, milnacipran)
Practice sleep hygiene for better rest
Try cognitive behavioral therapy for pain management
Pace activities to avoid overexertion
Use heat therapy and gentle massage for pain relief
emergency: Seek care if pain suddenly worsens or changes character
See a doctor if depression becomes severe or suicidal thoughts occur
